questions.question: Fair value is 18. You are long 4 units (you hold too much). To reduce your inventory you should generally:
options: Make your ask more attractive so buyers lift you and you sell
Make your bid more attractive so sellers hit you
Leave both quotes unchanged because inventory is irrelevant
Pull your ask and quote only a bid

explanations.A: Correct. A more attractive (lower) ask invites buyers to lift you; each lift sells a unit and shrinks your long position back toward flat.
explanations.B: A better bid pulls in sellers, so you'd buy even more and grow your long position — the opposite of what you want.
explanations.C: Inventory drives your directional risk; ignoring it is how mock traders blow up when fair value moves against the position.
explanations.D: Removing the ask kills your only path to sell off the long; quoting bid-only would just add more length.

questions.question: Fair value is 30. You are short 3 units (you owe the market). To reduce your inventory you should generally:
options: Make your bid more attractive so sellers hit you and you buy back
Make your ask more attractive so buyers lift you
Remove your bid permanently
Do nothing — short inventory carries no risk

explanations.A: Correct. A more attractive (higher) bid invites sellers to hit you; each fill buys back a unit and covers your short toward flat.
explanations.B: A better ask pulls in buyers, so you'd sell even more and deepen your short — the wrong direction when covering.
explanations.C: Pulling the bid removes your only path to buy back and cover; you'd stay short with no way to flatten.
explanations.D: A short loses when fair value rises, so it absolutely carries risk that must be managed.

questions.question: You are long and want to skew your quotes to offload risk. Fair is 40. Which way should you shift your quote midpoint?
options: Below fair — shade both bid and ask down
Above fair — shade both bid and ask up
Keep it exactly at fair
Skew randomly — direction does not matter

explanations.A: Correct. Shading the quote below 40 cheapens your ask (attracting buyers who take your length) and lowers your bid (so you stop accumulating more).
explanations.B: Shading up cheapens nothing for buyers and raises your bid, which would accumulate even more long.
explanations.C: A symmetric quote at fair ignores the directional pressure of being long; you need to lean toward selling.
explanations.D: Skew direction is the whole point: a long must lean its quotes to encourage the relieving (selling) trade.

questions.question: You are long 6 units. Your inventory loses money if fair value:
options: Falls
Rises
Stays exactly flat
Only when the spread widens

explanations.A: Correct. A long is marked to fair, so a drop in fair value is a direct loss on every unit you hold.
explanations.B: Rising fair value helps a long — that is a gain, not the risk being asked about.
explanations.C: If fair never moves there is no mark-to-market loss on the position itself.
explanations.D: Spread width is separate from inventory P&L, which is driven by changes in fair value.

questions.question: Your position limit is ±4. You are long 4 (at the maximum). Fair is 25. What is typically forced?
options: You may only quote to buy more
Nothing changes — limits are advisory
You may only quote to sell (offer-only) until you reduce position
You must cross your own quote

explanations.A: Buying adds length, but you are already at the +4 ceiling, so taking more is forbidden.
explanations.B: A hard position limit forces a behavior change; quoting normally would let a seller push you past it.
explanations.C: Correct. At max long you cannot take on more length, so you quote one-sided to sell — only an ask — until inventory drops below 4.
explanations.D: A crossed market (bid above ask) is invalid and is never the response to hitting a limit.

questions.question: You are long 2 units at an average cost of 38. Fair value is now 40. Your mark-to-market P&L on the position is:

explanations.A: Correct. Mark to fair: (40 − 38) × 2 = +4.
explanations.B: This flips the sign. A long gains when fair exceeds cost and loses when fair is below cost.
explanations.C: This is the per-unit move only. You hold 2 units, so multiply the move by 2.
explanations.D: This over-counts your size — you hold 2 units, not 3.

questions.question: It is near the close of a trading game. You are long 7 with a limit of 8 and fair is unchanged. Your priority should be to:
options: Flatten before the bell — reduce the position even if you give up a little edge
Maximize spread capture on brand-new round-trips
Buy more to average down
Widen your quotes to infinity and stop trading

explanations.A: Correct. Inventory left at the close marks against you at settlement with no time to trade out, so reducing risk dominates squeezing the last bit of spread.
explanations.B: Chasing more round-trips near the close keeps (or grows) a risky position you may not be able to exit in time.
explanations.C: Adding to the position increases inventory risk right when you have the least time to manage it.
explanations.D: Refusing to trade leaves the open position untouched; you still carry it into settlement.

questions.question: You are long. You shift your quote from 99–101 to 98–100 (same spread). This skew:
options: Raises your ask, discouraging buyers
Leaves the midpoint unchanged
Lowers your ask, making it easier for buyers to lift you and offload your long
Creates a crossed (invalid) market

explanations.A: The ask fell from 101 to 100; it did not rise.
explanations.B: The midpoint moved from 100 down to 99, so it did change.
explanations.C: Correct. The ask drops from 101 to 100, so buyers are more likely to lift you — exactly what a long wants in order to sell down inventory.
explanations.D: 98–100 still has the bid below the ask, so it is a valid two-sided quote.

explanations.B: The midpoint moved from 55 down to 54, so it did change.
explanations.C: 53–55 still has the bid below the ask, so it is a valid two-sided quote.
explanations.D: Correct. The ask drops from 56 to 55, so buyers are more likely to lift you — exactly what a long wants in order to sell down inventory.

questions.question: You are short. You shift your quote from 99–101 to 100–102 (same spread). This skew:
options: Raises your bid, making it easier for sellers to hit you so you can cover
Lowers your bid, discouraging sellers
Leaves the midpoint unchanged
Creates a crossed (invalid) market

explanations.A: Correct. The bid rises from 99 to 100, so sellers are more likely to hit you — exactly what a short wants in order to buy back inventory.
explanations.B: The bid rose from 99 to 100; it did not fall.
explanations.C: The midpoint moved from 100 up to 101, so it did change.
explanations.D: 100–102 keeps the bid below the ask, so it is a valid quote.

questions.question: In market-making terms, holding 'long inventory' means you have:
options: Sold more than you have bought
A perfectly flat position
Bought more than you have sold (a net positive position)
A crossed quote

explanations.A: That describes a short position, the opposite of long.
explanations.B: Flat means zero net position, not long.
explanations.C: Correct. Net buying leaves you long — you own units and gain if fair value rises.
explanations.D: Crossed describes a quote where bid exceeds ask, not a position.

questions.question: Holding 'short inventory' means you have:
options: Bought more than you have sold
Exactly zero position
Two-sided quotes only
Sold more than you have bought (a net negative position)

explanations.A: That is a long position, the opposite of short.
explanations.B: Zero net is flat, not short.
explanations.C: Quoting style is unrelated to whether you are net short.
explanations.D: Correct. Net selling leaves you short — you owe units and gain if fair value falls.

questions.question: Being 'flat' means your net position is:
options: Zero
Maximum long
Maximum short
Whatever the limit allows

explanations.A: Correct. Flat means no net inventory, so you carry no directional mark-to-market risk.
explanations.B: Max long is the opposite of flat.
explanations.C: Max short is the opposite of flat.
explanations.D: Flat specifically means zero, not the limit.

questions.question: The primary purpose of skewing your quotes is to:
options: Predict the next direction of fair value
Encourage the trade that reduces your unwanted inventory
Always widen the spread as much as possible
Guarantee a profit on every fill

explanations.A: Market making earns the spread; skew manages inventory, it is not a directional bet.
explanations.B: Correct. Skew leans prices so the relieving trade (selling when long, buying when short) becomes more attractive.
explanations.C: Skew is about shifting quotes directionally, not simply widening them.
explanations.D: No quoting choice guarantees profit; skew manages risk.

questions.question: When your inventory is flat and fair value is F, the ideal quote midpoint is usually:
options: Well below F
Well above F
At fair value F
Irrelevant — midpoint never matters

explanations.A: Shading below fair is what you do when long, not when flat.
explanations.B: Shading above fair is what you do when short, not when flat.
explanations.C: Correct. With no inventory pressure you center on fair to earn the spread symmetrically.
explanations.D: Centering on fair is exactly how a flat maker captures unbiased spread.

questions.question: Skewing to manage inventory should be thought of as:
options: Panicked dumping at any price
Refusing to quote at all
A directional bet on price
A measured price shift to invite the relieving trade

explanations.A: Skew is a controlled adjustment; firesale dumping gives away edge unnecessarily.
explanations.B: You still post prices when you skew; pulling quotes is not skewing.
explanations.C: Skew responds to your position, not to a forecast of direction.
explanations.D: Correct. You move enough to reduce risk without donating edge on every fill — it is deliberate, not panic.

questions.question: When you are flat, your main source of edge as a market maker is usually:
options: Capturing the bid-ask spread on round-trips
Favorable inventory marks
Guessing market direction
Avoiding all trading

explanations.A: Correct. A flat maker earns by buying at the bid and selling at the ask repeatedly.
explanations.B: When flat you hold no inventory, so there is nothing to mark — spread is the edge.
explanations.C: Directional guessing is not the market maker's structural edge.
explanations.D: You must trade to earn the spread; sitting out earns nothing.

questions.question: A 'flattening trade' is a trade that:
options: Always increases your position
Reduces your net position toward zero
Only changes the spread width
Crosses the market

explanations.A: That would be the opposite — building inventory, not flattening it.
explanations.B: Correct. Selling when long or buying when short moves your inventory back toward flat.
explanations.C: A flattening trade changes your position; spread width is a separate decision.
explanations.D: Crossing the market is invalid and unrelated to flattening.

questions.question: You are too long. To stop adding to the position, your bid should be made:
options: More attractive (raised toward fair)
Crossed above your ask
Less attractive (lower or wider away from fair)
Removed and then re-added at the same level

explanations.A: A higher bid attracts sellers and would buy you even longer — wrong way.
explanations.B: A crossed bid is invalid and would only cause errors.
explanations.C: Correct. A less attractive bid means fewer sellers hit you, so you stop accumulating more length.
explanations.D: Re-posting at the same level does not change the incentive to keep buying.

questions.question: You are too long. To encourage buyers to take your inventory, your ask should be made:
options: Much higher
Equal to your bid
Removed entirely
More attractive (lower / tighter)

explanations.A: A higher ask discourages buyers — you would stay long longer.
explanations.B: A zero-width quote is unusual and not the standard way to offload length.
explanations.C: Without an ask you cannot sell at all, so you cannot reduce the long.
explanations.D: Correct. A cheaper ask invites buyers to lift you, selling down your long.

questions.question: You are too short. To stop adding to the short, your ask should be made:
options: Less attractive (higher or wider away from fair)
More attractive (lowered toward fair)
Crossed below your bid
Set exactly equal to fair

explanations.A: Correct. A less attractive ask means fewer buyers lift you, so you stop selling more.
explanations.B: A cheaper ask attracts buyers and would sell you even shorter — wrong way.
explanations.C: A crossed ask is invalid.
explanations.D: Pinning the ask at fair still leaves it attractive enough to keep selling.

questions.question: You are too short. To encourage sellers to let you cover, your bid should be made:
options: Much lower
More attractive (higher / tighter)
Equal to your ask
Removed entirely

explanations.A: A lower bid repels sellers — you would stay short longer.
explanations.B: Correct. A higher bid invites sellers to hit you, buying back your short.
explanations.C: A zero-width quote is unusual and not the standard way to cover a short.
explanations.D: Without a bid you cannot buy, so you cannot cover the short.

questions.question: How far you skew your quotes should mainly depend on:
options: The color of the trading screen
Always exactly one tick regardless of position
The size of your inventory and how urgently you need to reduce it
Whatever maximizes spread width

explanations.A: Cosmetic factors have nothing to do with quoting.
explanations.B: A fixed one-tick skew ignores how much risk you actually carry.
explanations.C: Correct. Bigger or riskier inventory warrants a larger skew to pull you flat faster.
explanations.D: Skew is about leaning direction to manage inventory, not maximizing width.

questions.question: Why do trading games impose position limits?
options: To guarantee every trader a profit
To eliminate the bid-ask spread
To prevent any losses from ever occurring
To cap the risk a single trader can take on from inventory

explanations.A: Limits constrain risk; they do not guarantee profit.
explanations.B: Limits have nothing to do with removing the spread.
explanations.C: Limits cap risk but cannot prevent losses entirely.
explanations.D: Correct. Limits bound how much directional exposure inventory can create.

questions.question: Inventory risk is best described as:
options: The risk that fair value moves against you while you hold a position
The risk that the spread is too tight
The risk of quoting on both sides
The certainty of losing money on every trade

explanations.A: Correct. The longer and larger you hold inventory, the more an adverse price move can cost.
explanations.B: Spread width is a separate concern from holding-period price risk.
explanations.C: Two-sided quoting is normal; inventory risk is about the position you carry.
explanations.D: Inventory risk is a probability of adverse moves, not a certainty of loss.

questions.question: All else equal, carrying a large inventory increases your risk because:
options: Larger positions always earn more spread
A given move in fair value produces a larger P&L swing
Big positions are immune to price moves
The exchange pays you to hold inventory

explanations.A: Spread is earned on round-trips, not by simply holding a big position.
explanations.B: Correct. P&L scales with size, so bigger inventory amplifies the impact of any price move.
explanations.C: The opposite is true — bigger positions are more exposed to moves.
explanations.D: There is no such payment; holding inventory is a risk, not a reward.

questions.question: If you believe fair value mean-reverts, accumulating a large long after price has risen sharply is risky mainly because:
options: Mean reversion guarantees the price keeps rising
Long positions cannot lose money
Mean reversion suggests the price may fall back, hurting your long
Spread capture disappears when you are long

explanations.A: Mean reversion implies pull-back toward the mean, not continued rises.
explanations.B: Longs lose when fair value falls; they are not risk-free.
explanations.C: Correct. Under mean reversion an elevated price tends to revert downward, which marks your long against you.
explanations.D: You can still earn spread; the issue here is directional mark risk under reversion.

questions.question: Under mean reversion, being short after a sharp price drop is risky because:
options: Shorts always profit when price falls further
Short positions are immune to price moves
You can no longer quote a bid
The price may revert upward, marking your short against you

explanations.A: Mean reversion implies a bounce, not further falls.
explanations.B: Shorts lose when fair rises — they are exposed.
explanations.C: You can still quote; the risk here is reversion against the short.
explanations.D: Correct. A depressed price tends to bounce back toward the mean, which hurts a short.

questions.question: You are flat at fair 60, then a single big buy lifts you and leaves you short 4. The most consistent next move is to:
options: Skew your quote up so sellers hit your bid and you cover
Skew your quote down to sell even more
Leave the quote centered at 60
Pull both sides and stop quoting

explanations.A: Correct. Being short, you lean prices up to make your bid attractive and buy back toward flat.
explanations.B: Selling more deepens the short — the wrong direction.
explanations.C: A symmetric quote ignores the new short pressure.
explanations.D: Pulling quotes leaves the short open with no way to cover.

questions.question: You are flat at fair 60, then a single big sell hits your bid and leaves you long 4. The most consistent next move is to:
options: Skew your quote up to buy even more
Skew your quote down so buyers lift your ask and you sell
Leave the quote centered at 60
Pull both sides and stop quoting

explanations.A: Buying more deepens the long — the wrong direction.
explanations.B: Correct. Being long, you lean prices down to make your ask attractive and offload toward flat.
explanations.C: A symmetric quote ignores the new long pressure.
explanations.D: Pulling quotes leaves the long open with no way to offload.

questions.question: You are long 6 and fair has been drifting down all session. Compared with staying long, flattening now:
options: Increases your exposure to the down move
Has no effect on your risk
Reduces further loss if the downward drift continues
Locks in a guaranteed profit

explanations.A: Flattening removes exposure; it does not increase it.
explanations.B: Moving toward flat materially reduces directional risk.
explanations.C: Correct. Cutting the long removes exposure to additional declines in fair value.
explanations.D: Flattening caps risk; it does not by itself guarantee a profit.

questions.question: You buy 2 at 38 then sell 1 at 42; fair is 40 and unchanged. On the one remaining long unit your unrealized P&L vs fair is:

explanations.A: This double-counts; only one unit remains open, and its move is 2, not 4.
explanations.B: Fair (40) is above your cost (38), so the open unit is not flat in P&L.
explanations.C: The sign is reversed — fair is above cost, so the long is in profit.
explanations.D: Correct. One unit held at cost 38 marked to fair 40 gives 40 − 38 = +2 (separate from the spread already locked on the round-tripped lot).

questions.question: Skewing your midpoint below fair while keeping the same spread is the natural response when you are:
options: Long and want to offload
Short and want to cover
Flat and want symmetric edge
At your short limit

explanations.A: Correct. A long shades the quote down to cheapen the ask and stop accumulating on the bid.
explanations.B: A short shades up, not down.
explanations.C: When flat you center on fair, not below it.
explanations.D: At a short limit you lean up to buy back, not down.

questions.question: Skewing your midpoint above fair while keeping the same spread is the natural response when you are:
options: Long and want to offload
Short and want to cover
Flat and want symmetric edge
At your long limit

explanations.A: A long shades down, not up.
explanations.B: Correct. A short shades the quote up to raise the bid and stop selling on the ask.
explanations.C: When flat you center on fair, not above it.
explanations.D: At a long limit you lean down to sell, not up.

questions.question: You are long 5 with a limit of 5. A seller is resting, eager to hit your bid. The safest quoting choice is to:
options: Show an aggressive bid to capture the seller
Widen both sides symmetrically and keep bidding
Stop showing a bid and offer only, so you cannot be pushed past the limit
Cross your quote to force a trade

explanations.A: Buying from that seller would push you past your +5 limit.
explanations.B: Any live bid still risks being hit and breaching the limit.
explanations.C: Correct. At the long limit you must not accept more length, so you pull the bid and quote one-sided to sell.
explanations.D: Crossed quotes are invalid and never the right response to a limit.

questions.question: Two makers hold the same long, but one holds it for ten minutes and the other for ten seconds. All else equal:
options: The shorter holder faces more inventory risk
Holding time has no effect on inventory risk
Neither faces any risk because both are long
The longer holder faces more inventory risk

explanations.A: Less holding time means less exposure to adverse moves, not more.
explanations.B: Holding-period exposure is the core of inventory risk.
explanations.C: Both face risk; being long is not risk-free.
explanations.D: Correct. The longer you hold a position, the more opportunity there is for fair value to move against it.
